title: All India Students' Federation v. University Grants Commission
holding: University refusal to hold student union elections in compliance with the Lyngdoh Committee guidelines violates students' right to participate in democratic processes as part of their right to education under Article 21A and the right to association under Article 19(1)(c).
principle: Democratic participation in educational institutions is formative of the civic culture that the Constitution seeks to nurture; suppression of student representative bodies deprives students of a constitutionally recognised dimension of their educational experience.
summary: Students across multiple central universities challenged persistent administrative deferrals of student union elections citing order concerns. The Court, relying on the Lyngdoh Committee Report which it had itself commissioned, directed all universities receiving UGC funding to hold student union elections annually in accordance with the guidelines and made non-compliance a ground for withholding UGC grants.

title: Kali Prasad Sah v. State of Bihar
holding: A state government's deliberate policy of leaving teaching posts vacant in government primary schools, resulting in single-teacher schools unable to comply with Pupil-Teacher Ratio norms under the RTE Act, violates children's fundamental right to education.
principle: The right to education is a fundamental right enforceable against the state; the state's financial convenience cannot justify systemic non-compliance with minimum quality standards prescribed by Parliament.
summary: An educationist petitioned after survey data revealed that over 12,000 Bihar government primary schools operated with a single teacher for all classes, violating the RTE Act's PTR of 30:1. The Court held that RTE's PTR norms are judicially enforceable, directed filling of all teaching vacancies within one academic year, and appointed the State Education Commissioner as an officer of the court for compliance reporting.

title: Apna Ghar Foundation v. State of Haryana
holding: The state is constitutionally liable for the trafficking and sexual exploitation of children occurring within state-funded and state-supervised shelter homes; supervisory failure at such facilities is a direct violation of Article 23 and Articles 21 and 39(e).
principle: State-funded institutions for vulnerable children stand in loco parentis; the constitutional duty to protect children from exploitation is not dischargeable by delegating management to private NGOs without effective oversight.
summary: A women's rights foundation petitioned after an investigation revealed systematic sexual exploitation of minor girls in a state-funded shelter home, with local administration aware but inactive. The Court held the state directly liable, directed CBI investigation of all shelter home staff and supervisory officials, awarded interim compensation to victims, and directed a nationwide audit of all government-funded children's shelter homes.

title: Bombay Bar Association v. Ministry of Law and Justice
holding: Excessive court fees that effectively price out litigants of modest means from seeking civil remedies constitute a denial of access to justice and are unconstitutional to the extent they exceed what is necessary to defray administrative costs.
principle: Access to courts is a fundamental facet of the rule of law and the right to life; the state cannot use court fees as a revenue mechanism that renders civil justice inaccessible to the poor.
summary: The Bar Association challenged the Bombay High Court's schedule of ad valorem court fees applicable to commercial suits, which resulted in fees of several crore rupees on larger property disputes, effectively confining commercial civil litigation to wealthy parties. The Court held that court fees must be proportionate, not confiscatory, directed the Law Commission to recommend a revised fee structure on a cost-recovery basis within one year, and capped fees for non-commercial civil suits in the interim.

title: Centre for Child Rights v. Ministry of Women and Child Development
holding: Employment of children under 14 in any hazardous occupation, including domestic service and roadside eateries, is an absolute constitutional prohibition under Article 24 that applies regardless of whether the child's family consents or economic necessity is pleaded.
principle: Article 24's prohibition is categorical and non-derogable; poverty of the family and the child's apparent willingness are constitutionally irrelevant to the question of violation.
summary: An NGO petitioned following raids across three cities that found over 2,000 children under 14 employed as domestic workers and dhaba helpers, many transported from distant states in conditions of debt bondage. The Court declared such employment unconstitutional, directed immediate rescue and rehabilitation under the National Child Labour Project, awarded compensation from the Child Labour Rehabilitation cum Welfare Fund, and directed states to establish hotlines and district-level nodal officers for child labour reporting.

title: Dr. Y.S. Jagan Reddy v. Central Vigilance Commission
holding: The Central Vigilance Commission's advice on disciplinary matters involving corruption is advisory to the appointing authority, but systematic disregard of CVC recommendations by ministries without recorded reasons amounts to an arbitrary exercise of disciplinary power.
principle: Institutional advice from a constitutionally appointed vigilance body must be engaged with on merits; blanket rejection of CVC recommendations without recorded reasons renders disciplinary disposals arbitrary and judicially reviewable.
summary: A petitioner challenged the exoneration of senior government officers in corruption cases despite CVC recommendations for major penalties, arguing that ministries routinely overrode CVC advice without explanation. The Court directed that all future departures from CVC recommendations be accompanied by written reasons filed with the CVC, directed retrospective review of 50 recent departure cases, and referred the broader issue of CVC institutional empowerment to a three-member expert committee.

title: Forum Against Caste Atrocities v. State of Tamil Nadu
holding: The two-tumbler system and other practices of untouchability in tea stalls and public eating places are violators of Article 17 and the Protection of Civil Rights Act; local authorities permitting such practices are liable for dereliction of constitutional duty.
principle: Article 17 abolishes untouchability absolutely and in all its forms; the state's duty to enforce this abolition is active, not passive — silence in the face of continuing practice constitutes state complicity.
summary: An advocacy group documented the widespread practice in rural Tamil Nadu tea stalls of serving Dalit customers in separate vessels kept on the ground while upper-caste customers used regular cups. The Court held each documented instance a violation of Article 17, directed state prosecution of stall owners under the PCR Act, made district collectors personally responsible for eliminating the practice in their districts within six months, and ordered surprise audits by district-level monitoring teams.

title: Rajendra Singh Rana v. Uttarakhand State Election Commission
holding: Gram Sabhas must be convened at the frequencies and for the purposes prescribed by state Panchayati Raj Acts; administrative cancellation or indefinite deferral of Gram Sabha meetings violates the constitutional guarantee of participatory local democracy.
principle: The Gram Sabha is the foundational institution of constitutional local governance; it is not a consultative formality but a decision-making body whose convening is a constitutional obligation of panchayat functionaries.
summary: Residents petitioned after the Gram Sabha of their village had not been convened for over two years, causing vital welfare scheme approvals and social audit functions to lapse. The Court held that failure to convene the Gram Sabha at the statutory frequency violates Articles 243A and 243G, directed the Panchayat President to convene a meeting within 15 days under the supervision of the District Magistrate, and issued state-wide directions for mandatory quarterly Gram Sabha meetings with mandatory social audit agendas.

title: Irom Sharmila v. Union of India
holding: Parliament's failure to review the Armed Forces (Special Powers) Act in light of sustained judicial findings of its use to perpetrate extrajudicial killings over six decades is constitutionally unacceptable; the Court constitutes a permanent oversight committee to monitor AFSPA-related deaths and report to it annually.
principle: Legislation that has been judicially found repeatedly to facilitate gross violations of the right to life must be subject to meaningful periodic review; the doctrine of constitutional stasis does not protect laws that have become instruments of systematic rights violation.
summary: A petition by the iconic civil rights activist and former Manipur MLA sought repeal or fundamental reform of AFSPA following decades of documented atrocities. While stopping short of striking down the Act, the Court held that the state's failure to investigate AFSPA-related deaths is a pattern of unconstitutional conduct, constituted a three-member judicial oversight committee, directed annual AFSPA impact reports to be laid before Parliament, and set a five-year review deadline for the Court to assess whether the oversight regime had been effective.

title: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
holding: While Parliament has the power to amend any part of the Constitution under Article 368, it cannot amend or destroy the 'basic structure' or framework of the Constitution.
principle: The basic structure of the Constitution is inviolable; judicial review is a part of this basic structure.
summary: This landmark case involved a challenge to the 24th, 25th, and 29th Amendments. A 13-judge bench held that the power to amend does not include the power to abrogate the core identity of the Constitution.

title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
holding: The right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty under Article 21; any law depriving a person of liberty must not only be a 'procedure established by law' but must also be just, fair, and reasonable.
principle: Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not mutually exclusive; they form a golden triangle of fundamental rights.
summary: The petitioner's passport was impounded without giving reasons. The Court expanded the scope of Article 21 to include procedural due process and natural justice.

title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India
holding: The Right to Privacy is a fundamental right protected under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution.
principle: Privacy is the core of human dignity and liberty.
summary: A 9-judge bench unanimously declared privacy as a fundamental right, overruling MP Sharma and Kharak Singh. It established the triple test of legality, necessity, and proportionality for state intervention.

title: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
holding: Section 377 of the IPC, insofar as it criminalises consensual sexual acts between adults in private, is unconstitutional.
principle: Constitutional morality must prevail over social morality; sexual orientation is a natural attribute of privacy and dignity.
summary: The Court decriminalised consensual homosexual acts, holding that the classification under Section 377 was arbitrary and violated the right to equality and dignity.

title: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
holding: Article 21 only covers 'procedure established by law' and not 'due process'; fundamental rights are separate and not linked.
principle: Limited interpretation of personal liberty in the early years of the republic.
summary: One of the first major cases on fundamental rights. While largely overruled by Maneka Gandhi, it remains a critical baseline for understanding Indian constitutional evolution.

title: Minerva Mills Ltd. v. Union of India
holding: Parliament's power to amend the Constitution is limited; judicial review is a part of the basic structure.
principle: Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles are two wheels of a chariot; neither should be sacrificed for the other.
summary: The Court struck down sections of the 42nd Amendment which sought to give precedence to DPSP over Fundamental Rights and restrict judicial review.
